ai_landscape.body: India's Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) constituted the AI Governance and Economic Group (AIGEG) as the country's central institutional mechanism for AI governance policy development and coordination — operating as an apex inter-ministerial body supported by a Technology and Policy Expert Committee (TPEC), a standing expert advisory body chaired by the Secretary of MeitY and composed of experts spanning academic research, technology industry, and digital policy. The institutions were established following the release of India's AI Governance Guidelines at the AI Impact Summit 2026, which articulated a principle-based framework anchored in 'seven Sutras' and recommended the creation of AIGEG, TPEC, and an AI Safety Institute (AISI). AIGEG will coordinate whole-of-government AI governance and serve as the apex policy development node, while TPEC ensures deliberations are informed by global technological developments, regulatory design, and India's social and strategic context. For Prosus portfolio companies operating in India — including Brainly, Eruditus, and GoStudent — the AIGEG/TPEC structure signals a shift from advisory frameworks to institutionalised governance: regulatory expectations for AI transparency, risk assessment, and ethical deployment will increasingly be formalised through AIGEG-issued policy instruments.

ai_landscape.title: Brazil MGI Issues AI Governance Policy Ordinance 3,485/2026 for Federal Public Administration
ai_landscape.body: On 28 April 2026, Brazil's Ministry of Management and Innovation in Public Services (MGI) published Portaria No. 3,485/2026 in the Diário Oficial da União, establishing a comprehensive AI governance policy for the development, procurement, and use of AI applications across the ministry and its ColaboraGov shared services centre. The ordinance sets principles of transparency, human rights protection, security, privacy, human supervision proportional to identified risks, and digital sovereignty; creates a governance structure comprising senior administration, a Digital Governance and Information Security Committee, and a dedicated AI sub-committee; explicitly restricts generative AI tools to public information only, prohibiting processing of confidential, personal, or sensitive data; and mandates that public servants remain responsible for critically reviewing AI-generated outputs before administrative use. The ordinance enters into force 60 days after publication (circa late June 2026). While sector-specific in scope, it establishes the governance template being rolled out across Brazilian federal agencies and prefigures obligations that will apply once the national AI bill (PL 2338/2023, approved by the Senate in December 2024 and under review in the House) eventually becomes law. For Prosus operating in Brazil, this signals an accelerating public-sector AI governance framework that will shape procurement requirements and compliance expectations for AI-enabled services sold to government.

ai_landscape.title: New York RAISE Act Finalised: Frontier AI Safety Law Takes Effect January 1, 2027 with 72-Hour Incident Reporting
ai_landscape.body: On 27 March 2026, New York Governor Kathy Hochul signed the chapter amendment finalising the Responsible AI Safety and Education (RAISE) Act — New York's landmark law regulating frontier AI model developers — with an effective date of 1 January 2027. The RAISE Act requires large frontier model developers to develop and publish safety frameworks, conduct pre-deployment safety evaluations, notify the New York Department of Financial Services within 72 hours of identifying a qualifying safety incident (compared to California TFAIA's 15-day timeline), and protect whistleblowers; a new DFS oversight office will evaluate developer compliance and authorise the attorney general to seek civil penalties up to $1 million per initial violation and $3 million for subsequent violations. The Act applies to developers of frontier models 'developed, deployed, or operating in whole or in part' in New York, creating broad territorial reach. The law creates direct tension with the Trump administration's March 2026 National AI Legislative Framework, which proposes federal preemption of state AI laws. For Prosus and its AI provider partners — particularly OpenAI and Anthropic — this represents a second major US state-level frontier AI safety regime (alongside California's TFAIA) requiring safety protocol documentation and incident response pipelines to be operational before January 2027.

ai_landscape.title: EU AI Act August 2026 Enforcement: What Applies on 2 August and Why Businesses Cannot Wait for Omnibus
ai_landscape.body: The EU AI Act Service Desk confirmed that on 2 August 2026, the majority of the AI Act's rules come into full application, including: high-risk AI systems listed in Annex III enter into application; Article 50 transparency obligations (requiring disclosure to users that they are interacting with AI and machine-readable marking of AI-generated content) begin to apply; regulatory sandboxes must be established in every Member State; and enforcement starts at both national and EU level. The EU AI Office simultaneously activates enforcement powers over general-purpose AI model providers — meaning GPAI providers who have not complied with obligations applicable since 2 August 2025 (technical documentation, transparency, copyright summaries, systemic risk assessments) face enforcement exposure from that date. Administrative fines of up to €35 million or 7% of global annual turnover apply to the most serious breaches. Without the Digital Omnibus formally adopted before 2 August 2026, there is no legally operative delay. For Prosus, this creates an immovable compliance reference point: all AI-enabled products and services deployed in EU markets must be classified for risk, with Annex III systems audit-ready and Article 50 disclosures implemented no later than 2 August 2026.

ai_landscape.title: UK Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 Reshapes Automated Decision-Making Regime; ICO AI Code of Practice Forthcoming Summer 2026
ai_landscape.body: The UK's Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 came into force on 5 February 2026, replacing Article 22 of the UK GDPR with a permission-based model for automated decision-making (ADM) — shifting from a default prohibition with narrow exceptions to a positive permission framework that allows broader lawful use of ADM while imposing new safeguarding and notification obligations. A public consultation on ADM guidance ran until 31 March 2026, with final ICO guidance expected in summer 2026; the ICO is additionally developing a statutory code of practice on AI and ADM as part of its AI and biometrics strategy, which will provide the operative compliance framework for organisations deploying AI in decision-making contexts in the UK. The Act also contains provisions on AI training data and copyright that intersect with the parallel AI copyright consultation ongoing in the UK. For Prosus portfolio companies using AI-assisted decision systems in UK markets — including automated assessment, personalised learning, or automated content moderation — the shift to a permission-based ADM model and the forthcoming ICO AI Code of Practice represent the most immediate UK AI compliance horizon, requiring review of existing ADM lawful bases and communication obligations before the Code is finalised.

ai_landscape.title: EU AI Act Enforcement Structure Confirmed: AI Office Directly Enforces GPAI Rules; National Authorities Take High-Risk Systems
ai_landscape.body: A March 2026 European Parliament Think Tank brief on AI Act enforcement confirmed the hybrid model under which enforcement will operate from 2 August 2026: the EU AI Office holds direct enforcement power over general-purpose AI model providers — including the ability to request information, mandate evaluations and red-teaming, impose interim measures, and levy fines — while national market surveillance authorities are responsible for high-risk AI systems deployed in their territory, including systems in Annex III categories such as employment, education, law enforcement, and financial services. The AI Office's direct enforcement mandate for GPAI is the first instance of a supranational EU body holding primary enforcement jurisdiction over an AI product category. Council negotiating positions in the Digital Omnibus also clarified that national authorities should retain responsibility for specific sectors including law enforcement, border management, and financial institutions even where GPAI models are the underlying technology. For Prosus, this dual-channel enforcement architecture means portfolio companies must map each AI deployment against two separate compliance tracks: GPAI provider obligations (EU AI Office jurisdiction) and high-risk system obligations (national authority jurisdiction), with different documentation, conformity assessment, and notification requirements applying to each.

ai_landscape.title: UK Ada Lovelace Institute Calls for Substantive AI Regulation as AI Growth Lab Consultation Closes
ai_landscape.body: The Ada Lovelace Institute published analysis of the UK government's AI Growth Lab — a regulatory sandbox mechanism proposed under the AI Opportunities Action Plan — warning that the Lab as currently designed risks accelerating market entry for inadequately tested AI products rather than generating shared governance knowledge, and that it contrasts sharply with public opinion: 89% of UK adults surveyed favour establishing an independent AI regulator and agree AI products should not be deployed until proven safe. The Institute argues the Lab should prioritise UK SMEs and start-ups over technology giants, build shared regulatory infrastructure and evaluation capability, and produce clarity and capacity for the entire regulatory ecosystem rather than bespoke exemptions. The consultation referenced closed in late 2025, with the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology evaluating responses. For Prosus portfolio companies in the UK, the Ada Lovelace Institute's position represents a strong civil society and policy signal that the UK's pro-growth AI sandbox approach will face sustained scrutiny — and that any future statutory AI regulator, if recommended by Parliament, would likely impose assessment obligations before deployment that currently do not exist under the UK's sector-led approach.

ai_landscape.title: Singapore Model AI Governance Framework for Agentic AI Sets Global Benchmark for Autonomous System Accountability
ai_landscape.body: Singapore's Personal Data Protection Commission released its Model AI Governance Framework for Agentic AI in January 2026, providing a comprehensive governance structure for AI systems capable of autonomous multi-step task execution, and has since been adopted as a reference point by businesses and regulators across Asia-Pacific. The framework identifies four core governance themes: assessing and bounding risks upfront (including evaluation of autonomy scope and potential cascading effects), ensuring meaningful human accountability (with clear responsibility assignment and approval checkpoints), implementing technical controls (sandboxing, safety testing, privilege escalation protection), and promoting end-user responsibility (training, transparency, and the ability to deactivate agents). Unlike prescriptive legal mandates, the Framework operates as a compliance template that regulators in the region — including India's MeitY and, increasingly, the EU AI Office in its GPAI guidance — are beginning to incorporate into binding or quasi-binding instruments. For Prosus portfolio companies building or integrating agentic AI tools — particularly in enterprise SaaS, HR-tech, and edtech contexts — aligning with the Singapore Framework constitutes both best-practice risk management and proactive positioning against emerging regulatory requirements across the Group's APAC footprint.
